rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Q1 2026 Threat Report Records Highest Incident Count in Twelve Months
rows.summary:
The National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has published its Q1 2026 quarterly threat overview, finding that more than 70 cyber security incidents were recorded between January and March 2026, the highest figure in the preceding twelve months.

On 22 April 2026, NÚKIB published its first-quarter threat overview for 2026, setting out incident statistics, threat actor activity and situational assessments relevant to Czech entities. The report carries no financial penalties and does not concern a specific regulated entity.

NÚKIB finds that the rise in incident volume was driven primarily by a wave of DDoS attacks in January carried out by pro-Russian hacktivist groups, combined with a relatively elevated number of incidents classified under the intrusion category. NÚKIB records that, of the total incidents logged, seven were classified as significant, with the majority of those affecting state institutions, while all remaining incidents fell into the less-significant category. NÚKIB further finds that, although DDoS attack numbers declined progressively after January, successful intrusions remained at consistent levels throughout the quarter, encompassing compromises of systems, edge devices, databases, e-mail accounts and individual user accounts.

NÚKIB states that no ransomware attack was recorded against any entity subject to higher-obligation requirements under the new Cybersecurity Act during Q1 2026. The authority notes that ransomware incidents did affect providers of regulated services operating under lower-obligation requirements, with those cases coordinated through the National CERT (CSIRT.CZ). NÚKIB additionally reports a notable increase in March in the number of devices on Czech territory compromised by the REDHEBERG botnet, exploiting inadequately secured VNC services exposed to the internet, with over 3,200 compromised devices still recorded at the time of the report's publication.

On the broader threat landscape, NÚKIB identifies several incidents without direct impact on Czech national security but with potential indirect relevance to Czech entities, including a global cyber-espionage campaign conducted via the Signal application, extensive use of an advanced iPhone compromise tool designated as Coruna, and a spear-phishing cyber-espionage campaign against European states exploiting a vulnerability in Microsoft Office software. The report also notes that in March, NÚKIB participated in the NATO Cyber Champions Summit and hosted the Prague Cyber Security Conference 2026.

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted guidelines clarifying the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes under the GDPR and approved updated Europrivacy certification criteria as a European Data Protection Seal.

On 21 April 2026, the European Data Protection Board adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes. The Board also accepted two opinions on Europrivacy certification criteria sets.

The EDPB finds that scientific research requires clarification and provides six key orientating factors to determine whether processing qualifies as such: a methodological and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; research objectives; and the potential to contribute to or apply existing scientific knowledge in new ways. The Board determines that where research activities meet all six factors, they may be regarded as scientific research, and where they do not, the controller is required to justify and demonstrate why the activities nevertheless qualify.

The EDPB clarifies that further processing for scientific research purposes is considered compatible with the original purpose of collection, meaning controllers are not required to conduct a purpose compatibility test under the GDPR. The EDPB determines that controllers may rely on broad consent where research purposes are not fully known at the time of data collection, provided researchers adhere to applicable ethical standards and implement additional safeguards. The Board also clarifies that individual rights, including the right to erasure and the right to object, may be subject to restrictions where processing is necessary for scientific research.

On Europrivacy, the EDPB adopted Opinion 14/2026, approving an updated set of Europrivacy certification criteria as a European Data Protection Seal pursuant to Article 42(5) GDPR. The scientific research guidelines are open for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

rows.title: Czech National Cybersecurity Authority Launches EU-Funded Project to Support NIS2 Implementation
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cybersecurity Authority (NÚKIB) has officially launched an EU-funded project to support the implementation of the NIS2 Directive, financed through the European Commission's Technical Support Instrument and delivered by the Estonian e-Governance Academy.

On 15 April 2026, NÚKIB formally inaugurated the project titled 'Implementation of the EU Cybersecurity Directive NIS2' at a ceremony attended by the Estonian Ambassador Jana Vanaveski, NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr, and representatives of the European Commission and the e-Governance Academy (eGA). The project is funded by the European Union via the Technical Support Instrument (TSI), administered by the Task Force for Reform and Investment (SG REFORM) under the European Commission. NÚKIB states that no financial penalty is associated with this announcement; the project involves grant-based EU technical assistance with no stated monetary value disclosed in the source.

NÚKIB states that NIS2 Directive implementation has increased the number of regulated entities in the Czech Republic by at least fifteenfold, from approximately 400 to around 6,000. Adam Kučínský, Director of the NÚKIB Regulation Department, notes that the Directive affects nearly all sectors of the economy, including healthcare and the financial sector, both of which the authority identifies as dependent on secure and resilient digital infrastructure. NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr states that the scale of expansion in regulated entities makes it essential for the authority to have adequate organisational capacity and operational tools to carry out effective supervision across the enlarged scope of obligations arising under NIS2 (Directive (EU) 2022/2555).

The project is delivered by eGA in cooperation with the European Commission and is designed to strengthen NÚKIB's institutional and administrative capacities, supporting adaptation of its organisational structures and service delivery. NÚKIB states that planned outputs include sectoral analysis, current cybersecurity threat analysis, supply chain security support, change management support, and capacity-building activities. eGA Senior Expert and Project Team Leader Taimar Peterkop states that the project's objective is to translate NIS2 requirements into functional organisational processes, strengthen internal capacities, and support effective cooperation with regulated entities to enable NÚKIB to manage the extended scope of the Directive.

rows.title: NÚKIB Publishes Guidance on Documentation Requirements for Cloud Computing Catalogue Registration
rows.summary:
The National Cybersecurity Authority of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has published a supporting guidance document titled 'Guide to Evidencing Requirements for Registration of a Cloud Computing Service', directed at cloud computing service providers seeking entry into the national cloud computing catalogue under Decree No. 505/2025 Coll.

On 20 April 2026, NÚKIB published the Guide to assist cloud computing service providers in navigating the documentation requirements established by Decree No. 505/2025 Coll. on certain requirements for registration in the cloud computing catalogue. The Decree entered into force on 1 January 2026 and, as NÚKIB notes, sets out the technical and security requirements that providers are required to meet in order to register their cloud computing services in the catalogue.

NÚKIB states that the Guide is intended to improve comprehension of the applicable requirements and to assist providers in preparing complete and accurate applications at the point of first submission. The authority notes that the document introduces providers to key terminology, the legal framework, and the general principles of the Decree, and identifies the most frequent deficiencies that NÚKIB finds lead to delays or complications in the registration process.

NÚKIB states that the Guide further addresses the requirements applicable to individual cloud computing security levels. NÚKIB states that these level-specific requirements are set out in separate documents created for each security level individually, providing providers with clear support material when preparing documentation corresponding to the specific security level of the service being offered. NÚKIB has made the Guide and the accompanying security-level documents available on its regulatory support materials webpage at nukib.gov.cz.

rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Co-Seals Three-Part Guidance Series on Modern Defensible Architecture with Australian Signals Directorate and International Partners
rows.summary:
The National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic (NÚKIB) has co-sealed three guidance publications on modern defensible architecture alongside the Australian Signals Directorate and a coalition of international cybersecurity authorities, providing baseline design principles, senior decision-maker guidance, and investment planning frameworks for organisations seeking to strengthen their cyber resilience.

On 30 October 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency of the Czech Republic announced its co-sealing of the publication series, which carries no fine or penalty element. Co-signatories include the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security, the National Cyber Security Centre of New Zealand, the Japan Computer Emergency Response Team, Japan's National Cybersecurity Office and National Police Agency, the National Intelligence Service of the Republic of Korea, and Germany's Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik.

The series comprises three distinct publications. The first, Foundations for Modern Defensible Architecture, sets out a baseline collection of secure design and architecture activities intended to prepare organisations to adapt to current and emerging cyber threats. NÚKIB notes that each Foundation represents an organisational goal or capability intended to facilitate adoption of zero trust architecture and technologies. The second publication, Modern Defensible Architecture for Senior Decision Makers, is directed at executive-level audiences and addresses the contemporary threat landscape, presenting key factors and questions for consideration prior to investment in and implementation of modern defensible architecture. The third publication, Investing in Modern Defensible Architecture, targets ICT managers and enterprise architects, and NÚKIB describes it as a resource for developing an investment roadmap to present to those responsible for cyber security and information technology investment decisions.

Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, states that the documents represent an important step toward enabling organisations to practically and proactively design and build IT environments that minimise risks to critical systems. NÚKIB further states that the fundamentals of modern defensible architecture, together with the recommendations on decision-making and investment planning contained in the series, are consistent with the long-term security standards the agency promotes. The full publication series is hosted on the Australian Signals Directorate's website.

rows.title: NÚKIB Director Visits Australia for Bilateral Cyber Security Consultations
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) conducted its first official leadership visit to Australia in October 2025, holding bilateral consultations with Australian counterpart institutions across Canberra and Sydney on topics including critical infrastructure threats, artificial intelligence and secure technologies.

On 15 October 2025, NÚKIB confirmed that Director Lukáš Kintr and Roman Račka, Director of the Cabinet, undertook a working visit to Australia to advance bilateral cooperation in cyber security. The visit, described by NÚKIB as the first official visit of the agency's leadership to Australia, included engagement with representatives across several Australian government bodies.

In Canberra, the NÚKIB delegation held consultations with the Australian Cyber Security Centre, which operates under the Australian Signals Directorate. A bilateral meeting took place with the National Cyber Security Coordinator, Lieutenant General Michelle McGuinness CSC. The delegation also participated in a roundtable at the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, chaired by Hugh Watson, Acting Ambassador for Cyber Affairs and Critical Technologies, and met with senior officials from the Department of Home Affairs and the Department of Cyber Security. The official programme in Canberra concluded with a visit to the Australian Strategic Policy Institute and a reception for the Australian cyber community hosted at the Czech Embassy, opened by Ambassador Jana Tyrer.

The delegation subsequently visited Sydney to observe the operations of the Joint Policing Cybercrime Coordination Centre, led by the Australian Federal Police. NÚKIB notes that the Centre operates as a model of inter-agency collaboration between law enforcement and the private sector. Discussions across the visit addressed threats to critical infrastructure from state-sponsored actors, the security implications of artificial intelligence development, trustworthy technology standards, the protection of energy infrastructure and the security of connected vehicles. NÚKIB Cyber Attachée for the Indo-Pacific region, Veronika Kolek Netolická, noted that the visit represents a step towards deepening a partnership established when the Czech Republic appointed its first Cyber Attaché to the region in 2023, and referenced the NATO Cyber Champions Summit, which the Czech Republic is scheduled to host in 2026.

rows.title: Czech Government Approves National Cyber Security Strategy Effective 2026, Led by NÚKIB
rows.summary:
The Czech Government has approved a new National Cyber Security Strategy, effective from 2026, prepared by the National Cyber and Information Security Agency and replacing the strategy in force since 2021.

On 3 September 2025, the Czech Government formally adopted the new National Cyber Security Strategy (NCSS), with the National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) identified as the lead authority responsible for the document. No financial penalties are associated with this measure.

NÚKIB states that the strategy was developed with contributions from dozens of organisations across both the public and private sectors, including through public consultations. The agency notes that the NCSS responds to a deteriorating global security environment and recent technological changes, and that it emphasises the need for more intensive cooperation at national and international levels. NÚKIB further states that implementation will be carried out through an action plan currently being prepared, which will assign specific tasks to responsible institutions. The implementation of those tasks is to be evaluated annually and the results submitted to the Czech Government.

NÚKIB identifies three main strategic areas: securing strategic infrastructure, including strengthening resilience and enhancing the state's ability to detect and counter cyber threats; whole-of-society preparedness and development, covering digital competences, the cyber security workforce, and innovation; and international cooperation, encompassing an active role for Czechia within the EU, NATO and other international organisations. According to NÚKIB, Czechia is required in the coming years to make greater use of new technologies to secure organisations, prepare for potential transitions to crisis states, and improve working conditions for cyber security experts in public administration. The agency notes that the strategy also identifies the need to develop secure alternatives to risky technologies, build new platforms for information sharing, and pursue an internationally coordinated approach to deterring malicious state actors. NÚKIB Director Lukáš Kintr stated that the strategy confirms Czechia's capacity to respond to the current security environment and noted that threats and vulnerabilities must be detected and addressed in advance, with Russia and China named as examples of malicious state actors.

rows.title: Czech NÚKIB Joins International SBOM Cybersecurity Guidance Issued by CISA, NSA and 16 Partner Authorities
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) has joined a joint international guidance document on Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for cybersecurity, co-authored with the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), the U.S. National Security Agency (NSA), and fifteen additional national authorities across Europe, Asia-Pacific and North America.

On 3 September 2025, the full title of the document, "A Shared Vision of Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for Cybersecurity", was published under TLP:CLEAR, meaning disclosure is not limited and distribution carries no foreseeable restriction. NÚKIB notes that it has been an active member of the Global SBOM Forum, within which the document was developed, on behalf of the Czech Republic since early 2025. No financial penalties or enforcement measures are associated with this publication.

According to NÚKIB, the document presents SBOM as a machine-readable record of all components and libraries used in software development, functioning as a supply chain transparency mechanism. The authoring organisations state that widespread SBOM adoption is directed at producers, procuring organisations, and operators of software, with the stated aim of strengthening security, reducing risk, and decreasing costs. NÚKIB notes that when a vulnerability is identified in a component, SBOM enables rapid identification of its location and the taking of targeted remediation measures, with direct relevance to the protection of critical infrastructure.

The guidance further emphasises, according to NÚKIB, that SBOM adoption is an essential step toward aligning software development with the secure-by-design principle, under which security is integrated from the outset of development. According to the document, there is a call for the alignment of technical standards across countries and sectors to ensure interoperability and consistent large-scale implementation. Lukáš Kintr, Director of NÚKIB, is quoted in the authority's announcement as characterising SBOM as key to producing secure and resilient software, noting that modern software frequently consists of hundreds of components from varied sources and libraries, and that SBOM introduces transparency into that environment for both citizens and institutions.

rows.title: Czech National Cybersecurity Authority Issues High-Level Warning on Data Transfers and Remote Administration Linked to People's Republic of China
rows.summary:
The Czech National Cyber and Information Security Agency (NÚKIB) has issued a formal cyber security warning, rated High, concerning the transfer of system and user data to the People's Republic of China and the remote administration of technical assets carried out from PRC territories, pursuant to Section 12(1) of Act No. 181/2014 Coll. on Cyber Security.

On 3 September 2025, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency published the warning under file reference 350-647/2025-E, addressed to all authorities and persons obliged to implement security measures under the Cyber Security Act. No financial penalty is associated with this warning. NÚKIB determines the threat level as High, meaning the threat is rated as likely to very likely, and NÚKIB finds that obliged persons are required to evaluate the threat within their risk management processes at that same level, in accordance with Section 5(1)(d) of Decree No. 82/2018 Coll. on security measures.

NÚKIB concludes that the transfer of system and user data to the PRC, to PRC Special Administrative Regions, or to entities based in those territories, as well as remote administration of technical assets performed from or by entities in those territories, constitutes a cyber security threat. The Agency states that its conclusion is based on facts established during the exercise of its powers, supplemented by unclassified and classified information obtained from domestic and foreign partners.

NÚKIB finds that the legal environment of the PRC directly affects the trustworthiness of companies subject to it. The Agency identifies several PRC legal instruments as contributing factors to the threat assessment. These include the National Security Law of 2015, which the Agency notes imposes a general duty on all Chinese citizens and organisations to assist state authorities in matters of state security. NÚKIB further identifies the National Intelligence Law of 2017, and the Agency notes that Article 7 of which requires every citizen and organisation to support national intelligence activities and maintain confidentiality on related classified matters. The Agency also references the Counter-Espionage Law of 2014, noting under Article 6 that it applies extraterritorially and that, owing to broad and vague definitions, PRC state authorities can require companies to provide almost any information, with companies holding de facto no control over the information they possess and no access to independent judicial review. NÚKIB additionally notes that a 2023 amendment to that law further expanded its scope.

rows.title: European Commission Opens Public Consultation on EU Action Plan for Hospital and Healthcare Cybersecurity; Czech NÚKIB Encourages Participation
rows.summary:
The European Commission has launched a public consultation on the European Action Plan for the Cybersecurity of Hospitals and Healthcare Providers, published on 15 January 2025, with the Czech National Cyber and Information Security Authority (NÚKIB) drawing domestic attention to both the consultation and a related advisory board recruitment process.

On 14 May 2025, NÚKIB published a notice informing Czech entities that the European Commission had opened the consultation, which remains open until 30 June 2025. NÚKIB notes that it does not administer the consultation process, directing interested parties to the Commission's own digital strategy pages for submission details.

According to NÚKIB, the Action Plan identifies gaps and challenges in the healthcare sector and responds to them through specific initiatives at EU level, alongside recommendations directed at member states for action at national level. NÚKIB states that the plan sets out objectives across four areas: building capacity to prevent cybersecurity incidents; improving information sharing and threat detection capabilities; improving incident response and recovery capabilities; and identifying means of deterring potential threat actors. NÚKIB notes that contributions submitted through the public consultation may influence the final form of recommendations addressed to both EU-level entities and member states, as well as the activities envisaged under the Action Plan.

Separately, NÚKIB states that the European Commission has published a call for applications to a newly established Health Cybersecurity Advisory Board, intended to assist the Commission and the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) in implementing the Action Plan, and to support public-private sector cooperation. NÚKIB states that members of the board will be expected to, among other things, identify and share best practices in cybersecurity and share information with healthcare entities. The deadline for applications to the advisory board was 23 May 2025. NÚKIB states that it welcomes and supports potential interest in membership from Czech entities, noting that participation in the board may contribute to the prioritisation and effective implementation of activities set out in the Action Plan.
